species: Puget Sound Tidal Bore -- deep rhythmic surge through narrow passage
sound_type: Deep rhythmic surge: tidal bore through narrow passage. Deception Pass (150m wide, 8-9 knots max), Tacoma Narrows (1600m wide, 5-6 knots), San Juan Islands passages (Current Pass, Cattle Pass, San Juan Channel). Acoustic mechanisms: turbulence at channel walls (broadband), standing wave formation (rhythmic hydraulic), subsurface turbulent radiation, current-driven impact on rocks/cliff faces. Frequency: 20-2000 Hz (sub-bass body vibration + bass roar + mid turbulence + upper-mid cavitation). Duration: 2-4 hours per tidal cycle at near-maximum current. Three nested rhythms: standing wave (5-30 sec), current approach/recession (20-40 min), tidal cycle (6 hours).
ecosystem: Narrow tidal passage, high-velocity tidal channel. Deception Pass State Park (2M annual visitors, WA most-visited state park). Puget Sound fjord geometry: 62m avg depth, 160km north-south extent. Spring tide range 3-4m at Deception Pass.
season: Tidal -- all seasons. Maximum acoustic event at spring tides (lunar-solar alignment). Seasonal variation in tidal range but bore occurs year-round.
milestone: TIDAL HYDROLOGY AT MAXIMUM. E=8 through sustained hydraulic mass (millions of kg/sec) over hours. Distinct from rock avalanche (degree 322): episodic brief vs. sustained rhythmic. Three nested temporal rhythms. Body vibration sub-bass component (20-100 Hz) at close proximity. Deception Pass: among highest tidal velocities in Puget Sound (8-9 knots).

coordinates.description: Deception Pass: 48.407°N 122.645°W. Whidbey Island / Fidalgo Island passage. SR 20 bridge 50m above channel. Tacoma Narrows: 47.267°N 122.551°W. Kitsap Peninsula / mainland. San Juan Islands: multiple passages throughout the archipelago.

acoustic_data.mass_flow: Deception Pass maximum: 5000-10000 m3/sec x 1025 kg/m3 (salt water) = 5-10 million kg/sec. Fundamentally different order of magnitude from any biological acoustic source.
acoustic_data.frequency: Sub-bass 20-100 Hz (body vibration); Bass 100-300 Hz (dominant roar); Mid 300-1000 Hz (wall turbulence); Upper-mid 1000-2000 Hz (cavitation, surface turbulence). Full coverage 20-2000 Hz.
acoustic_data.duration: 2-4 hours per tidal cycle at near-maximum current conditions. The E=8 of duration, not of peak intensity.
acoustic_data.rhythm: Three nested timescales: standing wave oscillation (5-30 sec), current approach/recession from maximum (20-40 min), tidal cycle (6 hours).
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8 through: (1) mass flow rate millions kg/sec; (2) sustained duration hours; (3) sub-bass body vibration component; (4) full spectral coverage 20-2000 Hz. Distinct from episodic E=8 (rock avalanche, degree 322).

nodes.title: Sustained vs. Episodic E=8
nodes.description: Rock avalanche (322): E=8 through brief peak intensity (30-90 sec). Tidal bore (323): E=8 through sustained hydraulic mass (2-4 hrs). Two E=8 events in consecutive degrees with fundamentally different temporal characters.

nodes.title: Hydraulic Mass as Acoustic Mechanism
nodes.description: 5-10 million kg/sec of water at 8-9 knots through a 150m constriction. The acoustic output is generated by mass in motion, not by biological behavior. E=8 is inevitable given the physical parameters.

nodes.title: Three Nested Rhythmic Timescales
nodes.description: Standing wave (5-30 sec) + current approach/recession (20-40 min) + tidal cycle (6 hr). The 'deep rhythmic surge' has structure: it is not noise but rhythmically organized hydraulic sound at three simultaneous timescales.

cross_references.relationship: E=8 + E=8 = E=16. Sustained pressure pairing: proto-grunge sustained heavy output vs. tidal bore sustained hydraulic pressure. Both E=8 through persistence, not peak.
Consecutive geophysics E=8 entries. Avalanche: episodic (30-90 sec). Tidal bore: sustained (2-4 hours). Two E=8 events with opposite temporal characters.
Earlier Puget Sound at E=9. Storm surge: maximum oceanographic event. Tidal bore: regular hydraulic maximum, all seasons. E=9 vs. E=8 = exceptional vs. regular maximum.
